short_description: Works praising buddhas, bodhisattvas, or points of doctrine.

This is by far the largest division of the Tengyur, containing no less than 2,626 works. The subdivisions follow the structure set out by Zhuchen Tsültrim Rinchen, the 18th century editor of the Degé Tengyur, in his catalog to the collection (Toh 4569). He classifies the divisions and their works from “higher” to “lower.” The initial division is into the four principal classes of tantra, together with a fifth section for works he classifies as common to all four, or with no definite affinity to a single class. The first (and very large) Unexcelled Yoga tantra class is further subdivided into four main groups, with further subdivision in some of them, and the fifth section is subdivided into the collections of which it is largely composed.
The Indian treatises explaining and commenting on the various Perfection of Wisdom sūtras
The treatises by Nāgārjuna, Āryaveva, Candrakīrti, Buddhapālita, Śāntideva, and other Indian masters on the Madhyamaka (Middle Way) philosophical system.
The Indian commentaries on a number of doctrinally important sūtras (other than those on the Perfection of Wisdom).
The treatises by Maitreya, Asaṅga, Vasubandhu, and others on Yogācāra-Cittamātra themes, buddha-nature, and other topics.
Formal, scholastic treatises including part of the early Sarvāstivādin Abhidharma canon and later commentaries.
Commentaries and treatises on the canonical Vinaya texts.
The literature recounting stories of the Buddha’s previous lives.
Advice from the great Indian Buddhist masters in the form of letters written to rulers and others.
The texts on Indian Buddhist epistemology, logic, and reasoning by Dignāga, Dharmakīrti, and others
Treatises on language, Sanskrit grammar, composition, rhetoric, poetics, lexicons, etc.
Secular Indian treatises on Āyurvedic medicine and pharmaceutics.
Indian treatises on the graphic and manual arts, including iconometrics.
Indian treatises on ethics and worldly wisdom.

A collection of miscellaneous Indian texts that do not fit into any of the other categories such as early Tibetan scholars' works, newly inserted various texts on sūtra, general sciences, dedication and aspiration prayers.
Historical and descriptive catalogue of the Degé Tengyur by its 18th century editor Zhuchen Tsültrim Rinchen.
